description: Baseline harness setup: dockerized XHTTP XMUX benchmark scaffolding failed to execute (exit 127); diagnosing autoresearch script invocation.
Baseline benchmark ran in docker, but the autoresearch checks integration reported a false non-zero result; removing auto-check wrapper and validating tests manually instead.
Baseline XHTTP XMUX benchmark harness: added dockerized splithttp XMUX microbenchmarks and autoresearch docs/scripts.

Optimize XHTTP XMUX hot path with per-manager random selection and in-place cleanup; add safer xpadding/uplink defaults, preserve query params in Referer padding, and cover the fixes with new splithttp tests.

Further optimize XHTTP XMUX by merging unusable-client sweeping, eligible-client selection, and warm-refill accounting into one locked pass; preserve the config-default and xpadding fixes while keeping splithttp tests green.
Trim another XHTTP XMUX hot-path branch by dropping a redundant pre-selection warm-refill scheduling check; benchmark improves again while splithttp tests stay green.
Replace XHTTP XMUX reservoir-style selection with a one-pass round-robin cursor over the compacted usable client slice; this removes per-eligible RNG work while keeping splithttp tests green.
Trim XHTTP XMUX lock-path overhead by replacing the defer-based unlock in GetXmuxClient with explicit unlocks on each return path; splithttp tests remain green.
Tried inlining/reordering XMUX usability checks inside the sweep loop to avoid helper overhead, but the benchmark regressed slightly; reverting this hot-path reshuffle.

Add a lazy XHTTP XMUX fast path: perform full pool sweeps on a bounded interval, use cursor-based selection for normal acquisitions, and lazily remove bad entries encountered on the hot path; splithttp tests stay green.
Tried precomputing the XMUX warm target to shave scheduleWarmRefill overhead, but the benchmark regressed slightly; reverting this micro-optimization.
Tried splitting the lazy XMUX picker into separate concurrency/no-concurrency paths to remove a branch from the hot path, but it regressed slightly; reverting.
Tried relaxing the lazy XMUX health-sweep interval from 250ms to 500ms, but the benchmark regressed slightly; keeping the more conservative interval.
Tried reordering XMUX usability checks to avoid IsClosed on obviously spent clients, but the benchmark regressed slightly; reverting.
Tried initializing XMUX nextSweepAt during manager construction to skip the first sweep-zero check, but it regressed slightly; reverting.
Tried caching clientCount/connectionLimit locals in GetXmuxClient to trim repeated len/field reads, but the benchmark was slightly worse; reverting.

Improve lazy XHTTP XMUX selection by checking OpenUsage before the heavier usability probe on the fast path; saturated clients are skipped immediately while bounded sweeps still clean stale entries, and splithttp tests stay green.
Tried simplifying the lazy XMUX sweep-deadline branch by relying on `now.Before(nextSweepAt)` alone, but the benchmark regressed slightly; reverting.
Tried removing the redundant non-empty slice check from the lazy XMUX fast-path loop condition, but the benchmark regressed slightly; reverting.
Tried caching the lazy fast-path client count locally inside pickAvailableClientLocked to avoid repeated len() calls, but the benchmark regressed slightly; reverting.

Track approximate XHTTP XMUX usable-count state incrementally on client creation/removal/exhaustion and reconcile it on sweeps, so the hot path avoids threading usableCount through picker returns and warm-refill checks; splithttp tests stay green.
Only call XHTTP XMUX warm-refill scheduling after tracked client-count changes instead of every GetXmuxClient call; this slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Reorder XHTTP XMUX GetXmuxClient around an early hot-path return when a selected client exists and the pool is already at its connection target; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Add a direct first-candidate fast path in the lazy XHTTP XMUX picker before falling back to the full scan; the extra miss-path duplication slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Specialize XHTTP XMUX around config-level reuse/request/deadline flags and cached normalized ranges so the usability probe can skip disabled limits; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Replace per-call XHTTP XMUX sweep deadline polling with a timer-armed sweepDue flag, so the hot path only touches time.Now when a sweep is actually due or reusable-deadline configs need it; add coverage for off-cursor closed-client cleanup across the scheduled sweep boundary.

Cap XHTTP XMUX warmConnections against the sampled max-connections limit once in the manager constructor and reuse that stored target directly in warm fill/refill checks, trimming more hot-path bookkeeping after the timer-armed sweep change.

Split XHTTP XMUX warm-refill into an inlineable needsWarmRefillLocked check plus a separate scheduler to avoid entering the goroutine-launch helper on the common no-refill path; this was slightly worse than the new baseline, so discard.

Specialize the XHTTP XMUX usability probe to skip LeftRequests and reusable-deadline checks unless those limits are actually configured, using the existing config pointers instead of broader cached flag plumbing; splithttp tests stay green.

Inline and specialize the lazy XHTTP XMUX hot-loop usability checks directly inside pickAvailableClientLocked to avoid the helper call and repeated config probes; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Cache only the configured XHTTP XMUX request-limit and reusable-deadline booleans on the manager so pickXmuxClientLocked and isUsableClientLocked can avoid repeated config-pointer checks; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Add a no-cleanup fast path to lazy XHTTP XMUX cursor scanning that uses simple linear loops and only falls back to the mutating cleanup loop when a stale client is encountered; this was slightly worse than the current best, so discard.

Inline the lazy XHTTP XMUX pickXmuxClientLocked logic directly into GetXmuxClient to remove that helper call and keep sweep/time handling in one place; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Track whether the XHTTP XMUX warm target is already full and only enter warm-refill scheduling when the pool is below target or no refill is in flight; this slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Split the lazy XHTTP XMUX picker into a simple no-deadline/no-request-limit fast path and a full path, so the common per-client scan avoids extra request/deadline checks while configured limits still use the stricter path; splithttp tests stay green.

Further specialize the lazy XHTTP XMUX fast path for managers without a concurrency cap, so the common warm-pool selection loop skips the OpenUsage atomic entirely while concurrency-limited or request/deadline-limited cases still use the stricter picker variants; splithttp tests stay green.

Move XHTTP XMUX lazy fast-path dispatch into pickXmuxClientLocked so it picks the specialized picker variant up front and avoids an extra wrapper call plus redundant now.IsZero/request-limit branching on every acquisition; splithttp tests stay green.

Add an even narrower XHTTP XMUX no-concurrency fast path that directly returns the cursor client when it is usable and only falls back to the cleanup loop when that cursor entry is stale; this slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Inline the XHTTP XMUX warm-refill guard directly at GetXmuxClient call sites so the common full-pool path avoids calling scheduleWarmRefillLocked at all; this slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Further specialize the XHTTP XMUX lazy picker for managers without CMaxReuseTimes by adding closed-only variants for the no-concurrency and no-deadline/no-request-limit paths, so common default pools skip leftUsage checks while stricter variants still handle configured reuse/request/deadline limits; splithttp tests stay green.

Precompute a compact XHTTP XMUX picker mode in the manager constructor and switch on that mode in pickXmuxClientLocked instead of rechecking config pointers each call; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

In the XHTTP XMUX concurrency-specialized picker variants, cache the positive concurrency limit locally and remove the redundant per-iteration `m.concurrency > 0` guard inside loops that only run when a concurrency cap is already known to exist; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Only schedule XHTTP XMUX warm refills when usable-count state actually changes (lazy removals, sweep removals, or reuse exhaustion) instead of calling scheduleWarmRefillLocked on every successful selection; splithttp tests stay green.

Normalize zero/disabled XHTTP XMUX reuse/request/deadline ranges to nil once in the manager config copy so pickXmuxClientLocked and isUsableClientLocked can use simpler nil-only dispatch checks; this regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Refactor lazy closed-client removal logging and slice cleanup into a shared slow-path helper to shrink the hottest specialized XHTTP XMUX picker bodies; this was slightly worse than the current best Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Replace the specialized XHTTP XMUX picker loops’ scanned-vs-clientCount bookkeeping with a remaining-counter form in the hot no-reuse and closed-only paths; this substantially regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Attempted to add a fast XMUX closed-state path by caching per-client atomic closed flags, but the first pass left mux.go and benchmark plumbing in an inconsistent build state; fixing the refactor and retrying.

Cache an optional per-client atomic closed-state pointer in XmuxClient so the hot picker paths can call a tiny isClosed helper instead of repeatedly dispatching through XmuxConn.IsClosed; benchmark mocks and internal closable conns were updated to exercise the same path, but the full Dockerized xmux benchmark was still slightly worse than the current best, so discard.

Cache the xmuxClients slice header locally inside the two hottest specialized XHTTP XMUX lazy pickers and refresh it only after lazy removals, so the common no-removal path avoids repeated manager field reads/indexing while preserving cleanup behavior; splithttp tests stay green.

Tighten the two hottest specialized XHTTP XMUX picker loops a bit further by using equality-only wrap checks and a local nextClientIndex assignment when advancing the round-robin cursor; this was slightly worse than the new best Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Add an immutable XHTTP XMUX client-snapshot fast path for the common no-request-limit/no-deadline/no-reuse cases, using an atomic round-robin cursor plus an atomic sweepDue flag so GetXmuxClient can often return without taking the manager mutex; preserve existing lazy cleanup and warm-refill behavior by falling back to the locked picker as soon as a scanned candidate is closed or the pool still needs growth. splithttp tests stay green.

Replace the immutable XHTTP XMUX fast-path snapshot transport from atomic.Value to a typed atomic pointer holding an immutable snapshot struct, so GetXmuxClient keeps the same lock-free snapshot behavior and fallback-on-closed semantics while avoiding interface load/type-assert overhead on every hot-path acquisition; splithttp tests stay green.

Tried folding the atomic sweepDue gate into the immutable XMUX snapshot itself by niling the fast snapshot while a sweep is pending, so tryGetXmuxClientFast could skip a separate atomic Bool load; this preserved correctness but slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Split the immutable XMUX fast path into separate no-concurrency and concurrency helpers so the dispatcher can skip one hot branch and pass a cached concurrency local into the stricter scan; this substantially regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Shrink the immutable XHTTP XMUX fast-path round-robin cursor from atomic.Uint64 to atomic.Uint32; the cursor only needs wraparound semantics for pool selection, so this trims the hot Add/modulo path without changing snapshot selection behavior or the locked fallback logic. splithttp tests stay green.

Cache the fast-path connections and concurrency fields in locals inside tryGetXmuxClientFast so the lock-free XMUX snapshot scan avoids a few repeated manager field reads; this slightly regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Specialize the no-concurrency branch of the immutable XMUX fast path down to a direct cursor lookup plus closed check, removing its now-redundant one-iteration scan loop; this was slightly worse than the current best Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Extend the immutable XHTTP XMUX fast snapshot with cached power-of-two metadata (clientCount, indexMask, powerOfTwo) and use a mask-based index path when the current snapshot size is a power of two, while preserving the existing modulo fallback for other pool sizes; splithttp tests stay green.

Publish a nil immutable XMUX fast snapshot when the pool is actually empty, so the hot path can use the atomic pointer nil check as the empty-pool gate and avoid carrying a redundant zero-client snapshot case; preserve the existing power-of-two and fallback selection behavior, with splithttp tests still green.

Only publish the immutable XMUX fast snapshot when the fast path is actually eligible: the pool is non-empty, already at its sampled connection target when bounded, and no request-limit / reusable-deadline / reuse-count configs force the slow path. This moves those gates out of tryGetXmuxClientFast and trims the hot path to the sweep flag plus snapshot load before selection; splithttp tests stay green.

Tried pre-seeding the immutable XMUX fast-path round-robin cursor to MaxUint32 so tryGetXmuxClientFast could use atomic.Add(1) directly without the current subtract-one adjustment, but the Dockerized xmux benchmark was a touch worse than the new best, so discard.

Cache an optional directly-readable closed flag on each XMUX client when the underlying connection exposes one, and use that in the immutable fast path before falling back to XmuxConn.IsClosed. Real DefaultDialerClient now exposes its existing atomic closed flag, internal closable XMUX test conns use the same path, and the benchmark fake keeps the same always-open behavior while exposing the common production fast path; splithttp tests stay green.

Track on each immutable XMUX fast snapshot whether every published client has a direct closed flag, and use dedicated fast branches that call closedFlag.Load() directly without a per-client nil/fallback check when that common case holds; keep the interface fallback for mixed snapshots, with splithttp tests still green.

Tried flattening tryGetXmuxClientFast so the power-of-two + all-closed-flags snapshot case becomes a single top-level hot branch before the more general paths, but the reshaped control flow was slightly worse than the current best Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried replacing the snapshot powerOfTwo/allClosedFlags bool metadata with a compact bitfield and hot-case flag comparisons in tryGetXmuxClientFast, but that extra snapshot-flags plumbing regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried loading the immutable XMUX fast snapshot before checking sweepDueFlag and combining the nil/sweep gate into one early return branch, but that reshaped entry path was slightly worse than the current best Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried flipping the fast-path concurrency scans from `>= continue` to a `< return` shape so the successful path falls directly into the closed check and return, but that branch reshuffle regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

In the immutable XMUX fast path’s power-of-two + all-closed-flags + concurrency-limited branch, replace the per-iteration (nextIndex+scanned)&mask recomputation with an incrementing masked index cursor; preserve selection order and fallback-on-closed semantics, with splithttp tests still green.

Revisited fast-path local concurrency caching in the new snapshot context by caching m.concurrency only inside the hottest power-of-two + all-closed-flags branch, but that narrower variant still regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried inverting the all-closed-flags fast-path closed check to return on `!closedFlag.Load()` instead of branching on the rare closed case first, but that reshaped branch direction regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Publish and consult a second immutable XMUX fast-snapshot pointer dedicated to the common power-of-two + all-closed-flags case, so tryGetXmuxClientFast can skip loading the general snapshot and skip the allClosedFlags/powerOfTwo dispatch checks on that hottest path while preserving the existing general immutable snapshot fallback for other eligible cases; splithttp tests stay green.

On the specialized immutable XMUX fast path for the common power-of-two + all-closed-flags case, cache the mask as an int local once per acquisition and use int(nextIndex)&mask for both the direct no-concurrency start index and the concurrency-limited masked cursor setup; preserve the specialized snapshot-pointer dispatch and splithttp tests stay green.

Tried splitting the specialized power-of-two + all-closed immutable XMUX fast snapshot into its own smaller typed snapshot struct with an int mask precomputed at publish time, but the extra specialized snapshot allocation/plumbing still regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried converting the specialized power-of-two + all-closed snapshot path’s fastNextClientIndex result to int immediately and reusing that int for both start/index masking, but that int-early conversion regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark versus the current best, so discard.

Tried reusing the already-computed specialized power-of-two start index as the concurrency-branch masked cursor seed instead of recomputing `int(nextIndex)&mask`, but that tiny reshuffle regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried caching the specialized immutable power-of-two snapshot mask as an int field on the general XMUX snapshot struct so the hottest path could skip the uint32-to-int conversion at acquisition time, but the larger snapshot metadata regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

Tried moving the specialized power-of-two + all-closed snapshot path’s start-index computation inside the no-concurrency branch so the concurrency branch only computes its masked cursor when needed, but that branch reshuffle regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark, so discard.

name: XHTTP XMUX broader workload including non-power-of-two pools

description: Broaden the XHTTP XMUX benchmark workload to reduce power-of-two overfitting by adding non-power-of-two warm-pool and concurrency-filter microbenchmarks (12-connection pools) alongside the existing 8/16-connection cases, then reset the autoresearch baseline on this wider harness; splithttp tests stay green.

On the broadened XMUX workload, tried collapsing the non-power-of-two + all-closed-flags + no-concurrency immutable fast path down to a direct cursor lookup instead of the existing single-iteration loop form, but that reshaped non-power-of-two branch regressed the Dockerized benchmark sum, so discard.

On the broadened XMUX workload, replace the immutable fast path’s non-power-of-two + all-closed-flags + concurrency-limited `start+scanned` wrap-adjust loop with an incrementing index cursor that only advances and wraps on saturated candidates; preserve fallback-on-closed behavior, and splithttp tests stay green.

Tried extending the broadened-workload specialization by publishing and consulting a second immutable snapshot pointer for the non-power-of-two + all-closed-flags case, but the extra pointer load/plumbing substantially regressed the Dockerized XMUX benchmark sum, so discard.

On the broadened workload, tried specializing the all-closed non-power-of-two case further by publishing and consulting a dedicated snapshot pointer for that branch, but the extra atomic pointer load/plumbing regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark sum, so discard.

On the broadened workload, tried replacing the non-power-of-two snapshot start-index modulo with a precomputed reciprocal fast-mod reduction using math/bits.Mul64, but that extra multiplier state and two-multiply reduction regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark sum, so discard.

Revisited the specialized power-of-two + all-closed immutable XMUX snapshot with a smaller dedicated snapshot struct carrying an int mask, but on the broadened workload that extra specialized snapshot allocation/plumbing still regressed the Dockerized xmux benchmark sum, so discard.

name: XHTTP transport broader workload including XMUX and request shaping

description: Broaden the XHTTP transport workload again to reduce XMUX-only overfitting by adding deterministic packet-up request-shaping benchmarks for FillPacketRequest across header, cookie, and body payload placement variants, update autoresearch.sh to include both XMUX and request-shaping benches, and reset the baseline on the wider xhttp_ns_sum workload; splithttp tests stay green.

Optimize the new request-shaping workload in FillPacketRequest by writing encoded payload chunks directly into request.Header for header placement and directly into request cookies for cookie placement, instead of first rebuilding an intermediate payload header/cookie collection and then merging it back into the request. This preserves chunking plus later metadata/xpadding behavior while lowering the broader xhttp_ns_sum workload; splithttp tests stay green.

Optimize the cookie-heavy FillPacketRequest path by writing the generated payload cookies directly into the request Cookie header when the normalized uplink-data key is already a valid cookie token, instead of calling request.AddCookie for every payload chunk. Session/seq/xpadding cookies still use the existing metadata path, preserving observable behavior while removing repeated payload-cookie sanitization/merge overhead; splithttp tests stay green.

Reduce packet-up request-shaping formatting overhead by replacing fmt.Sprintf-based payload chunk key generation with direct string concatenation plus strconv.Itoa in both the direct FillPacketRequest paths and the reusable payload header/cookie builders. This preserves the same header/cookie names while lowering the broader xhttp_ns_sum workload; splithttp tests stay green.

Speed up packet-up request shaping by reading request bodies with an exact-size fast path when ContentLength is already known, falling back safely for short or overlong bodies, and by caching the normalized uplink chunk-size range once per payload emission loop instead of recomputing it for every chunk. Apply the body-read fast path in both FillPacketRequest and the browser dialer packet sender; splithttp tests stay green.

Add safe direct-append fast paths for metadata/xpadding cookies and query params: append RFC-safe cookie pairs straight onto the request Cookie header instead of routing through repeated AddCookie sanitization/merge work, and append safe non-duplicate query params straight onto RawQuery instead of reparsing/re-encoding the full URL. Keep fallbacks for duplicate keys and unusual cookie values, and add tests covering duplicate-query replacement plus cookie visibility through request.Cookie; splithttp tests stay green.

Try avoiding request.URL.String plus raw URL reparsing on the request-side query-in-header xpadding path by building the Referer header from req.URL directly and passing an empty RawURL through FillStreamRequest/FillPacketRequest. This cut bytes/allocs on the body-placement benchmark, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed slightly, so discard.

Try streamlining GetRequestHeader by preallocating the header map, canonicalizing configured keys directly with textproto.CanonicalMIMEHeaderKey, and assigning single-value slices without going through Header.Add/Get. This kept semantics and secondary metrics flat, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed, so discard.

Try shaving the exact-size request-body fast path further by checking for one extra byte before falling back to io.ReadAll on the overlong case, and add coverage for short/long content-length mismatches. Secondary bytes improved a bit, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed, so discard.

Try moving fixed-range specialization out of hot request-shaping loops: when UplinkChunkSize or XPaddingBytes are configured with From==To, use the fixed value directly instead of re-entering the generic random-range path on every chunk/request. The extra branchy duplication regressed the broader xhttp_ns_sum workload badly, so discard.

Reduce cookie-heavy FillPacketRequest allocation churn by estimating the final payload Cookie header length from the encoded payload size, normalized chunk range, key, and any existing cookie prefix, then pre-growing the strings.Builder once before streaming payload chunks into it. This preserves the same chunk names/order and existing-cookie behavior while cutting cookie-path allocations; splithttp tests stay green.

Try trimming per-chunk cookie-builder formatting by writing payload chunk indices into the strings.Builder with a stack buffer plus strconv.AppendInt instead of strconv.Itoa string allocations. Secondary metrics stayed flat and the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed slightly, so discard.

Prototype a pooled small-body read path for FillPacketRequest so header/cookie payload placement can reuse request-body buffers instead of allocating a fresh slice per request. This run crashed at build time because the new pooled-body regression test was missing an io import; fix and retry.

Try reusing small packet-up request-body buffers from a sync.Pool in FillPacketRequest so header/cookie payload placement avoids allocating a fresh body slice for each request. The pool cut secondary bytes, but it regressed the broader xhttp_ns_sum badly under the mixed workload, so discard.

Try precomputing the payload key prefixes (`key-` / `key_`) once per request-shaping call so hot chunk loops only concatenate the numeric suffix. This badly regressed the broader xhttp_ns_sum workload despite unchanged secondary metrics, so discard.

Try caching a per-config base request-header template in a sync.Map and cloning it on each GetRequestHeader call, with a regression test to ensure callers still receive independent header copies. Despite the profile pointing at GetRequestHeader, the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed, so discard.

Try bypassing net/http Header.Set in the payload-header chunk loops by canonicalizing the uplink-data key once with textproto.CanonicalMIMEHeaderKey and assigning single-value slices directly into the header map. Added a regression test for lowercase custom keys, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum workload regressed badly, so discard.

Try avoiding request.URL.String when xpadding does not actually need a RawURL by teaching FillStreamRequest/FillPacketRequest to pass an empty requestURL into GetRequestPaddingConfig unless the effective placement is query-in-header. Added focused coverage for the placement decision helper. Secondary bytes/allocs improved slightly, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed, so discard.

Try streamlining appendCookieHeader for metadata/xpadding cookies by bypassing Header.Set, pre-growing a builder for the final Cookie string, and rebuilding the header value directly from the existing Cookie slice plus the new pair. It reduced secondary bytes/allocs slightly, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed sharply, so discard.

Try trimming GetRequestHeader overhead by preallocating the header map and tracking whether User-Agent was already supplied with strings.EqualFold during the configured-header copy, so the function can skip the trailing Header.Get probe on every request. This regressed the broader xhttp_ns_sum workload badly, so discard.

Reduce repeated chunk-index formatting in request shaping by precomputing decimal strings for the first 64 payload chunk indices and reusing them across header/cookie payload emission plus cookie-header size estimation, falling back to strconv.Itoa only for larger chunk counts. This preserves header/cookie names exactly while shaving a bit more hot-loop string work from both payload placements; splithttp tests stay green.

Speed up query-in-header xpadding by reusing setURLQueryParam inside ApplyXPaddingToHeader after URL parsing, instead of always round-tripping through u.Query plus q.Set plus q.Encode. This preserves existing replacement semantics through the helper fallback while letting the common safe append case skip a full query re-encode, cutting the mixed xhttp workload further; splithttp tests stay green.

Try caching repeat-x padding strings for lengths up to 1024 so GeneratePadding can reuse immutable strings instead of calling strings.Repeat on every xpadding request/response. This cut secondary bytes and allocs, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum regressed, so discard.

Retry a specialized immutable XMUX snapshot pointer for the all-closed-flags case, not just the power-of-two subset, so tryGetXmuxClientFast can skip the general snapshot load and allClosedFlags dispatch on more of the common path. The fresh combined profile justified a revisit, but the broader xhttp_ns_sum still regressed, so discard.

Try a server-side compatibility fix for the real intermittent 400 stream-one failures by widening implicit default xpadding validation so legacy-default and balanced-default peers accept each other's default padding ranges, and add focused tests reproducing those cross-profile requests. This likely addresses the user-reported 80-99 / >640 default-range mismatch, but under the current client-heavy xhttp benchmark segment it regressed the primary metric, so discard for now and revisit under a broader end-to-end workload.

name: XHTTP broader workload including server-side stream-one handling and compatibility

description: Broaden the XHTTP workload again to include server-side stream-one handling and the real balanced-client vs legacy-default-server compatibility case that was producing intermittent 400s in the field: add end-to-end-ish stream-one benchmarks for balanced→balanced and balanced→legacy-default handling, and make implicit default xpadding validation accept the union of legacy/balanced default ranges when xPaddingBytes is left unset so mixed-profile peers stop rejecting each other. Add focused tests for both directions and reset the baseline on this wider workload; splithttp tests stay green.

Speed up the newly added server-side stream-one / compatibility workload by teaching ExtractXPaddingFromRequest to scan the common safe query-in-header padding URL directly for the x_padding key before falling back to full url.Parse(...).Query().Get(...). Apply the same fast path to both the legacy Referer branch and the general configured header branch, while preserving the parse fallback for encoded or unusual inputs; splithttp tests stay green.

Tried shaving the broadened stream-one path with smaller helper cleanups: return early from ApplyMetaToRequest when both session and seq are empty, fast-path balanced GetResponseContentType when Accept is missing, and replace ExtractMetaFromRequest's strings.Split-based path parsing with a two-segment scanner plus added coverage for empty-path and mixed placement cases. Despite fewer allocs in the stream-one benches, the primary mixed xhttp workload regressed, so discard.

Speed up the broadened server-side stream-one path by trimming common ServeHTTP fallback overhead: add a cheap SplitHostPort+ParseIP fast path for request.RemoteAddr with ResolveTCPAddr fallback for unusual inputs, reuse the normalized xpadding range/method once per request, and bypass the generic response-padding helper stack for the common non-obfs response header case by writing X-Padding directly. Add IPv4/IPv6/hostname parsing coverage; splithttp tests stay green.

Trim more common request/stream-one helper overhead without changing semantics: in GetRequestHeader, fast-path the empty-custom-header case by returning only the default User-Agent directly; in balanced GetResponseContentType, return application/octet-stream immediately when Accept is absent instead of lowercasing/scanning an empty string; and reorder FillPacketRequest's balanced content-type guard so non-balanced body uploads skip an unnecessary header lookup. Add empty-Accept coverage; splithttp tests stay green.

Speed up query-in-header xpadding again by adding a raw-URL safe-append fast path in ApplyXPaddingToHeader: append x_padding directly onto already-normalized URLs, including fragment-preserving insertion before #..., and only fall back to url.Parse plus setURLQueryParam when the key already exists or the query needs full parsing. Add focused tests covering fragment preservation and duplicate-key replacement; splithttp tests stay green.

Tried specializing xpadding query writes further by adding a value-known-safe URL query helper for generated padding so ApplyPaddingToQuery and the parsed fallback in ApplyXPaddingToHeader could skip re-validating the generated padding string on every request. Despite being semantics-preserving for repeat-x/tokenish padding, the primary mixed workload regressed, so discard.

Tried a very small XMUX fast-path cleanup in tryGetXmuxClientFast: cache m.concurrency and snapshot client-count metadata in locals, reuse the first masked index in the dedicated power-of-two snapshot branch, and thread those locals through the hot loops to reduce repeated field loads. This unexpectedly regressed the primary mixed workload, so discard.

Tried reusing the caller's existing empty request.Header map on the common behavior=nil path instead of always allocating/replacing a fresh header map in FillStreamRequest and FillPacketRequest, plus added focused tests covering that empty-header reuse path. This cut bytes and allocs across the request benchmarks, but the primary mixed xhttp workload regressed versus the current best, so discard.

Tried replacing request.URL.String() in FillStreamRequest and FillPacketRequest with a common-case simple URL-string builder that fast-paths the normal scheme+host+path(+query/+fragment) XHTTP request form and falls back to url.URL.String() for opaque or unusual URLs. Added coverage for simple and opaque URL equivalence. Despite trimming some request-side allocation metrics, the primary mixed workload regressed, so discard.

Tried reshaping ServeHTTP control flow around the widened stream-one workload by routing directly on the existing sessionId/method/seq decision and moving scMaxEachPostBytes plus uplinkDataKey computation down into the packet-up-only branch, so stream-one requests skip unused packet-up normalization work. Despite helping the intended stream-one common path structurally, the primary mixed workload regressed versus the current best, so discard.

Tried a path-placement micro-optimization in ApplyMetaToRequest: when both session and seq use the default path placement, append both segments in one shot and skip the normalized key lookups plus second suffix check. Added focused path-append coverage. It reduced some bytes/allocs on the body request bench, but the primary mixed workload regressed, so discard.

Tried caching more request-handler normalization on the server side: add a requestHandler constructor that precomputes normalized xpadding range/method/response-padding config, cached uplink-data key plus post-size limit, and cached allow-credentials state so ServeHTTP can avoid repeating config normalization and use a handler-local response-header helper. This looked structurally sound for real traffic too, but it regressed the primary mixed workload, so discard.

Tried isolating the common no-metadata client path instead of the earlier broader regressing ApplyMeta bundle: skip calling ApplyMetaToRequest from FillStreamRequest when sessionId is empty, and from FillPacketRequest when both session and seq are empty, so stream-one requests avoid a useless metadata helper call. Despite being semantics-preserving and narrower than the prior discarded bundle, the primary mixed workload regressed, so discard.

Tried narrowing Content-Type work on the common behavior=nil stream request path: in FillStreamRequest, only probe balanced upload content types when a non-nil RequestBehavior is present, while keeping the legacy gRPC default behavior unchanged; in FillPacketRequest, guard the balanced upload-content-type path with behavior != nil as well. This reduced some helper work on stream-one requests but still regressed the primary mixed workload versus the current best, so discard.

Tried narrowing GetRequestPaddingConfig's obfs-mode work by populating only the placement fields actually used by each xpadding placement: skip normalized header lookup for query/cookie placements, skip key lookup for plain header placement, and set Method once up front. Despite being a small helper-level cleanup on hot request paths, the primary mixed workload regressed, so discard.

Tried extending the direct cookie payload builder in FillPacketRequest so that, when all extra cookie placements are safely appendable, it also appends xpadding/session/seq cookies directly into the same Cookie builder instead of first building payload cookies and then routing xpadding/meta through repeated appendCookieHeader/AddCookie work. Added coverage that the combined cookie path still leaves payload plus extra cookies visible through request.Cookie. This improved secondary bytes/allocs strongly, but the primary mixed workload still regressed versus the current best, so discard.

Tried replacing the per-acquisition XMUX closed-flag probes with close-notifier invalidation: connections that can notify on close now nil the fast snapshots and set sweepDue, letting the common all-notifier fast path return directly without atomic closedFlag.Load calls. Added coverage that a notifying off-cursor closed client triggers immediate sweep/refill instead of waiting for the periodic sweep. Despite being a profiler-backed real-path idea aimed squarely at the heavy atomic.Bool.Load cost, the widened mixed workload regressed versus the current best, so discard.

Tried a tiny helper-level fixed-range fast path in RangeConfig.rand: when From==To, return the single configured value directly instead of routing through crypto.RandBetween and its cross-package call overhead. This is semantics-preserving for all fixed XHTTP ranges (xpadding bytes, chunk sizes, sampled XMUX limits) and came much closer than recent tries, but the widened mixed workload still landed slightly worse than the current best, so discard.

Tried a narrower server-side meta-extraction fast path: in ExtractMetaFromRequest, skip strings.Split when the validated path suffix is empty, since stream-one requests with default path-based metadata often carry no session/seq segments at all. Added focused tests covering the empty-suffix path plus a mixed path+query case. This clearly improved secondary bytes/allocs in the widened workload, but the primary metric still regressed versus the current best, so discard.

Tried narrowing the appendCookieHeader hotspot highlighted by the request profile: avoid strings.Join plus Header.Set on the common Cookie-header cases by directly assigning the canonical Cookie slice, with a dedicated fast path for a single existing Cookie value and a fallback join only for multi-value Cookie headers. Added coverage that multi-value existing Cookie headers still keep all cookies visible through request.Cookie after metadata appends. Despite directly targeting a real request-path hotspot, the widened mixed workload still regressed slightly versus the current best, so discard.

Tried narrowing net/http header canonicalization on the xpadding/meta helper path by directly assigning already-canonical default header names (Referer, X-Padding, X-Session, X-Seq) instead of routing those common cases through Header.Set, while preserving Set for custom or non-canonical keys. This targeted the request profile's MIMEHeader.Set / CanonicalMIMEHeaderKey overhead, but the widened mixed workload regressed badly, so discard.

Tried a narrower fixed-range xpadding fast path after the earlier generic RangeConfig.rand attempt came close but regressed: add a helper used only by request/response xpadding generation so fixed-length xpadding configs return From directly instead of routing through crypto.RandBetween, without touching XMUX sampling or payload chunk-size selection. Applied it in GetRequestPaddingConfig and the server-side response-padding path. Despite being tightly targeted at real request/stream-one hotspots, the widened mixed workload still regressed, so discard.

Tried making XMUX health-sweep scheduling more asynchronous: remove the timer callback's manager lock by letting the callback only flip atomic sweepDue/sweepScheduled flags, and let the locked picker consult sweepDueFlag directly before sweeping. Added targeted internal coverage that scheduled sweeps also remove off-cursor request-limit-expired and reusable-deadline-expired clients, not just closed ones. Despite being a profiler-backed XMUX idea aimed at reducing timer-callback lock interference and tightening lazy-sweep coverage, the widened mixed workload regressed substantially, so discard.

Tried reducing the cookie-payload request-shaping base64 hotspot without changing chunk semantics: on the common valid-cookie-token path in FillPacketRequest, encode into a pre-sized []byte with base64.RawURLEncoding.Encode and stream those bytes directly into the Cookie builder, instead of first allocating a full encoded string and then slicing it back into chunk strings. This cut secondary bytes/allocs strongly on the cookie benchmark, but the widened mixed workload still regressed versus the current best, so discard.
